coredata.dc:title: Nanostructures derived from expired drugs and their applications toward sensing, security ink, and bactericidal material
coredata.dc:creator: Devi, Pooja
Jindal, Neha
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Thakur, Anupma
coredata.dc:description: In this research, a facile and economical route is introduced for the transformation of pharmaceutical waste (i.e., expired medicines) into value-added fluorescent carbon quantum dots (pharmaceutically derived CQDs abbreviated as ‘P-CQDs’). The synthesized P-CQDs were identified to have surface functionalities of –OH, C=O, and C=C with an average size of ~2–3 nm and a high quantum yield of 35.3%. The photoluminescence of P-CQDs recorded a maximum optical emission intensity at 2.8 eV (425 nm). The binding of Cu (II) ions by -COOH functionalities on the surface of P-CQDs led to its fluorescence quenching (turn-off) over a wide Cu (II) concentration range of 0.25–50 ppm. The P-CQDs exhibited the detection limit of 0.66 ppm (well below the WHO permissible limit of 2 ppm). The fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs-Cu (II) complex was recovered from NaHCO3.Hence, their “off-on” behavior was also explored for security ink applications for information encryption and decryption. Moreover, the rich oxygenated groups on the surface of the P-CQDs were utilized for green synthesis of plasmonic Ag@P-CQDs nanostructures, which were also demonstrated to have enhanced potential as bactericidal materials (e.g., against both E. coli and S. aureus). The overall results of this study are demonstrated to help create new and diverse routes for converting expired drugs into value-added nanostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Expired medicines
Carbon quantum dots
Cu (II) sensing
Security/fluorescent ink
Ag NPs
Bactericidal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: P-CQDs in metal ion sensing and security ink applications
Metal ion sensing and security ink applications

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to acknowledge the financial support of the Department of Science and Technology , India under SEED ( SP/YO/126/2017 ) and INSPIRE fellowship grants. KHK acknowledges the support made by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, & Future Planning (Grant No: 2016R1E1A1A01940995 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis method and mechanism of P-CQDs from GENPAS drug.

Optical characteristics of green synthesized P-CQDs: (a) UV–Vis absorption, (b) luminescence, (c) FT-IR, and (d) Raman spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Deconvoluted XPS core-level spectra of P-CQDs: (a) C (1s) core level and (b) O(1s) core level.
Structural characterization of P-CQDs: (a) HR-TEM image and (b) size distribution histogram.

Sensing response of P-CQDs toward Cu (II) ions: (a) PL spectra of P-CQDs in the presence of different inorganic ions; (b) ratio of fluorescence intensity (F/Fo) of P-CQDs in the presence of various studied metal ions; (c) response of P-CQDs against different concentrations of Cu (II) ions; and (d) Stern-Volmer plot.
PL spectrum of P-CQDs ink with addition of different concentrations of (a) Cu (II) and (b) sodium bicarbonate.

Photographic images of the inhibition zones of two types of bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus) wrt Ag@P-CQDs concentration: comparison between contrasting irradiation conditions (under light ((a) and (c)) vs. dark conditions ((b) and (d)) and their effects on optical density ((e) and (f)), respectively).
Comparison of analytical performance between different CQDs synthesized for Cu (II) ions sensing.

row.entry:
LR: 10 to 50 ppm;
LOD: 0.66 ppm; Wide detection range: 0.25–50 ppm

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Various carbonaceous nanostructures employed as fluorescent/security inks.

head.ce:title: Nanostructures derived from expired drugs and their applications toward sensing, security ink, and bactericidal material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh 160030, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh-160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad 201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad- 201002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: P. Devi, CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh 160030, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, a facile and economical route is introduced for the transformation of pharmaceutical waste (i.e., expired medicines) into value-added fluorescent carbon quantum dots (pharmaceutically derived CQDs abbreviated as ‘P-CQDs’). The synthesized P-CQDs were identified to have surface functionalities of –OH, C=O, and C=C with an average size of ~2–3 nm and a high quantum yield of 35.3%. The photoluminescence of P-CQDs recorded a maximum optical emission intensity at 2.8 eV (425 nm). The binding of Cu (II) ions by -COOH functionalities on the surface of P-CQDs led to its fluorescence quenching (turn-off) over a wide Cu (II) concentration range of 0.25–50 ppm. The P-CQDs exhibited the detection limit of 0.66 ppm (well below the WHO permissible limit of 2 ppm). The fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs-Cu (II) complex was recovered from NaHCO3.Hence, their “off-on” behavior was also explored for security ink applications for information encryption and decryption. Moreover, the rich oxygenated groups on the surface of the P-CQDs were utilized for green synthesis of plasmonic Ag@P-CQDs nanostructures, which were also demonstrated to have enhanced potential as bactericidal materials (e.g., against both E. coli and S. aureus). The overall results of this study are demonstrated to help create new and diverse routes for converting expired drugs into value-added nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, there are no unified or proper guidelines on how to handle expired pharmaceutical drugs due to the lack of regulatory policies and/or technological solutions. Consequently, these expired drugs often have been released into water bodies and are considered emerging water pollutants (Fram and Belitz, 2011; Kadam et al., 2016). Several formulations of antibiotics and estrogenic hormones used in the drug industry belong to the “Drinking Water Contaminant Candidate List” (Seehusen and Edwards, 2006). According to a recent antimicrobial resistant benchmark report, a cross-section of the pharmaceutical industry is responsible for the antimicrobial resistance threat due to their neglect in the area of drugs waste management, causing leakage of these drugs into the environment (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Wang et al., 2013; Wang and Hu, 2014; Capoor and Bhowmik, 2017; Glassmeyer et al., 2017).

As much as 1000,000 tons of pharmaceutical drugs are used for both the human and veterinary populations every year (Pierrat et al., 2015). However, improper discharge of drug waste into natural surroundings has been observed to cause devastating environmental and human health impacts (Shi et al., 2017). This is a result of the high biochemical activity of these pharmaceutical drugs in organisms even at very low concentration levels such as parts per billion (ppb) or parts per trillion (ppt).The globally practiced approaches for drug disposal are diverse and include chemical decomposition (Rogowska et al., 2019), waste encapsulation (Chu et al., 2010), cement kiln incineration (Kadam et al., 2016), and sanitary landfills (Peng et al., 2014), etc. Nonetheless, the performance of these disposal methods is being questioned, since these pharmaceuticals are being dumped into the environment as municipal solid waste, sewage, and gaseous waste (Boxall et al., 2012; Mohapatra et al., 2014). According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), pharmaceuticals can easily migrate into potable water (Boxall et al., 2012). Thus, contamination of groundwater and drinking water with pharmaceutical drugs can result in negative and chronic consequences for human health (Voudrias et al., 2012).

To seek a solution to this unexplored waste from the viewpoint of material science, we studied it as an economical source to synthesize novel (carbonaceous and plasmonic) nanostructures. The “Waste-to-Wealth” approach is taken into consideration not only for management of expired drugs (waste),but also for potential of a new precursor material for production of value-added nanostructures. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is the smallest member of the carbon family, known for its utility in energy harvesting, photocatalysis, bio-imaging, drug delivery, lasers, LEDs, photodetectors, and sensors, etc. (Lim et al., 2015; Sun and Lei, 2017; Chang and Tseng, 2018). CQDs are well known for characteristic properties of photostability, chemically inertness, cost-effectiveness, non-toxicity, eco-friendliness, biocompatibility, colloidal stability, and tunable photoluminescence (Zheng et al., 2015; Li et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018). Because of these characteristic properties, CQDs compete other metallic nanostructures such as semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), organic fluorophores, dyes, conjugated polymers, etc., in terms of high (aqueous) solubility, facile functionalization, and high resistance to photo-bleaching (Yang et al., 2009; Shenderova et al., 2002; Pan et al., 2020).In general, various types of precursors rich in carbon content (natural and synthetic) are used for synthesis of CQDs (Das et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018; Venkatesan et al., 2019). However, to the best of our knowledge, conversion of expired pharma-waste drugs into fluorescent CQDs has not been reported. Considering quantum yield, fluorescence, stability, and processing costs of CQDs, the proposed method in this study can compete with existing synthesis approaches based on carbon precursors viz. starch, glucose, sucrose, etc. (Yan et al., 2015; Dimos, 2016; Zu et al., 2017).

In this study, CQDs obtained from expired pharma drugs, abbreviated as pharma derived CQDs (P-CQDs), were synthesized from a model drug (i.e., GENPAS (generic name, consisting of paracetamol and dicyclomine hydrochloride salt) in light of its common availability) using a facile pyrolysis approach. The synthesis parameters were optimized, and the resulting P-CQDs were studied in detail for their optical, compositional, and structural properties. To demonstrate their potential utility, we also investigated them for applications including sensing, security ink formulation, and silver nanoparticle (Ag NP) synthesis. These multimodal applications were based on the switchable photoluminescence properties of P-CQDs and the presence of several surface functionalities (Jana et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2017). Ag-NPs are generally synthesized through the sodium borohydride reduction method, which is a chemical synthesis route with limitations (e.g., in terms of controlled reduction). Therefore, the resulting Ag-NPs are prone to aggregation due to weak stabilizers. On the other hand, due to the presence of -COOH and -OH groups on the surface, P-CQDs are not only involved in reduction of metal ions into NPs, but they also offer stability to resulting metallic NPs (Jana et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2017).

Silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) are the most sought-after material in biocidal applications due to their excellent antibacterial activities. Therefore, they are favored in various types of commercial applications such as wound healing creams, bandages, clothes, refrigerator/washing machines coatings, and water purifiers (Prabhu and Poulose, 2012; Tran and Le, 2013; Möhler et al., 2018). Furthermore, the obtained secondary material derived from P-CQDs (e.g., Ag@P-CQDs (silver decorated P-CQDs)) was shown to have bactericidal activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria in this study. In summary, the presented multimodal applicability of the obtained value-added materials, i.e., P-CQDs and Ag@P-CQDs, supports upscaling of the current study for management of expired drug waste.

An expired pharmaceutical drug, GENSPAS (dicyclomine hydrochloride and paracetamol salt), was procured from Aegis Elixirs Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, average M.W. 40,000) and sodium hydrogen carbonate were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. For Ag@P-CQD synthesis, silver nitrate and ammonia were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Ethanol and acetone (Merck) were used as a solvent for this study. Standard stock solutions (1000 ppm, Inorganic Ventures) of various ions including Cu(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), Zn(II), Mg(II), Li(II), As(III), and Co(II) were used for sensing studies with P-CQDs. Deionized water obtained from a Millipore Corporation water system (Milli-Q, 18.2 MΩ) was used in preparation of all reagents and standard solutions. For the bactericidal activity study, Luria Bertani Broth and Nutrient Agar were purchased from Hi Media Pvt. Ltd.

P-CQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing an expired GENPAS drug solution (52 mg/mL) based on its common availability under optimal conditions of 200 °C for 25 min. Expired drug tablets (520 mg each) were collected, crushed into a fine powder, and dissolved in 100 mL deionized water. The obtained solution was subjected to high-temperature pyrolysis in a hot-air oven set at 200 °C for 25 min (Fig. 1 ). A thick, brown colored residual was obtained and reconstituted with deionized (DI) water (10 mL), followed by purification through filtration and dialysis (MWCO-0.3 kDa) against water. In this synthesis process, no extra waste was produced, and the reconstitute dP-CQDs were stored at 4 °C until further use. The absolute quantum yield (QY) measurement for the P-CQDs was recorded using an integrated sphere-quantum yield assembly. For synthesis of the second class of nanostructures (i.e., Ag NPs), P-CQDs were used as reducing/capping agents. The presence of hydrophilic functionalities such as –OH and/or -COOH, on the surface of P-CQDs reduced Ag+ to Ag0, leading to formation of Ag@P-CQDs (Luo et al., 2012).In summary, P-CQD (3 mL, 0.5 M) solution was added into a mixture of silver nitrate (1 mL, 0.01 M) and ammonia (0.5 mL, 0.06 M), which was then stirred in dark conditions for 1 h at room temperature for Ag@P-CQD synthesis. The resulting yellowish-brown colored Ag@P-CQDs were dialyzed against water to remove unreacted precursors. The Ag@P-CQDs are not prone to aggregation as evidenced from their visual color. The aggregated Ag-NPs were reported to show a color change from yellow to dark yellow, then green, and finally to blackish-gray (Jornet-Martinez et al., 2019). However, no such changes were perceived in our study. Then, the resulting purified solution was stored in the refrigerator, characterized, and further studied for bactericidal activity.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of synthesized materials were recorded using a Hitachi U-3900-H spectrophotometer in a 200–800 nm wavelength range. The photoluminescence properties (i.e., emission and QY) of P-CQDs were probed with anFS5 Edinburgh spectrofluorimeter equipped with a xenon source and integrated sphere-quantum yield assembly. The surface functionalities of P-CQDs were characterized using FT-IR (Varian 670 IR) and X-ray photoelectron (Omicron) spectroscopy. Furthermore, the carbonaceous architecture of P-CQDs was studied with Raman (inVia instrument, Renishaw, λex = 785 nm) spectroscopy. A transmission electron microscope (H-7500, Hitachi Ltd., Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 250 kV was used to determine particle size and morphological characteristics of P-CQDs. The bright blue fluorescence of P-CQDs was captured under a UV trans-illuminator (epsTM) equipped with 305 and 365 nm UV lights. A minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) study was conducted with a BIO-RAD (iMark) microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: P-CQDs in metal ion sensing and security ink applications
ce:section.ce:para: To carry out sensing studies, P-CQDs were examined against a library of ions (1 ppm), such as Cu (II), Ni (II), Pb (II), Cd (II), Cr (III), Mg (II), K (I), and Co (II). These metal ions were added into the P-CQDs solution at 1:1, incubated, and instantly measured for the effects on the photoluminescence (PL)property of P-CQDs with a PL spectroscopy technique. With preliminary observations in terms of selectivity for Cu (II) ions over other metal ions, detailed sensing studies were carried out for Cu (II) ions. A wide concentration range (0.25–50 ppm) of Cu (II) ions was studied at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm to assess the dynamic working range of P-CQDs. For the security ink study, P-CQDs were mixed with PVP solution (1%, 5 mL) in a 1:2 ratio to prepare a polymeric formulation, which was subsequently stirred at room temperature for 40 min to yield a colorless, viscous ink. The as-prepared ink was dispensed into a fountain-ink pen refill, which was used for text writing on laboratory grade filter paper. The codes/words written with P-CQD-based ink were encrypted and decrypted with Cu(II)and NaHCO3 solutions, respectively, under UV light (365 nm).

The bactericidal activity of P-CQDs and Ag@P-CQDs was studied using both the agar well diffusion method and microplate reader method. First, cultures of E. coli and S. aureus (100 μL) were grown on agar plates (20 mL) with wells (8 mm) loaded at different concentrations of Ag@P-CQDs (0.19 M, 0.12 M, 0.08 M, 0.05 M, 0.03 M, and 0.01 M) and P-CQDs. The agar plates were incubated overnight in the light as well as in dark conditions due to a previous report of a light-activated response of CQDs (Wang and Hu, 2014). The bactericidal activity of the presented materials was interpreted in terms of zone of inhibition (in diameter). In addition, the MIC of Ag@P-CQDs was calculated using the 96-well microplate method with an ELISA plate reader.

Under pyrolysis, the natural carbonaceous compounds of expired/non-expired drugs, such as paracetamol and dicyclomine hydrochloride including gelatin, plasticizers, sugar, etc., sequentially underwent successive processes of dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization, followed by in-situ surface passivation under optimized synthesis conditions (Fig. 1). The presented method is facile, single-step, and fast and only requires inexpensive or expired drug tablets as carbon precursors. It is also a value-added solution for expired pharmaceutical drug management.

Fig. 2(a) presents the absorption characteristics recorded for the obtained P-CQDs. Broad absorption peaks were noted at ~330 nm (in the UV region) and were attributed to the n→π* and π→π* transitions associated with C=O and C=C molecular architecture, respectively, of the P-CQDs. The expired drug did not show any characteristic peak. On the other hand, Ag@P-CQDs exhibited an additional peak at 410 nm associated with the surface plasmon resonance band of Ag NPs (Saxena et al., 2011; Han et al., 2015) (Fig. S1(a)), which was confirmed using an XRD ω−2θ scan (Fig. S1(b)). The XRD spectrum showed the presence of strong diffraction peaks at 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.6°, and 77.5°, which were assigned to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of the Ag nanostructures, respectively (JCPDS Card Number 89-3697) (Tran and Le, 2013). In addition, a broad diffraction peak at ~22° was associated with the amorphous carbonaceous architecture (i.e., P-CQDs). Photoexcited emission studies (Fig. 2(b)) of P-CQDs portrayed the presence of broad emission characteristics (350–650 nm) with a maximum intensity centered at ~425 nm (λex = 340 nm). The observed excitation-independent emission behavior of the P-CQDs revealed the presence of uniform sp2 clusters within the P-CQDs architecture and overall size uniformity of the obtained P-CQDs from GENPAS (Zhu et al., 2015). The fluorescence of the as-synthesized P-CQDs was found stable over the time. The absolute QY of the obtained P-CQDs measured using the integrated sphere-quantum yield assembly was ~35.3% (ESI, Fig. S2). FT-IR spectrum analysis (Fig. 2(c)) revealed the presence of various functional groups with specific vibration peaks that were primarily assigned to C=C (~2100 cm−1) and -COOH groups (~1690 cm−1). The presence of these functionalities was responsible for the hydrophilicity and stability of P-CQDs in an aqueous environment (Gregoriou and Rodman, n.d.). Likewise, the presence of D and G bands at ~1372 cm−1 and ~1638 cm−1, respectively, in Raman spectrum analysis confirmed their carbonaceous structural framework (Fig. 2(d)) (Kouloumpis et al., 2017). The relatively higher intensity of the D band than that of the G band in the spectrum directed the increased degree of localized sp3 defects within the sp2 clusters (Sun et al., 2006). However, the expired drug did not show any characteristic peak in the Raman spectra.

To further elucidate the elemental information of the P-CQDs, XPS analysis was performed, and the deconvoluted core level spectra of C (1s) and O (1s) are presented in Fig. 3 . The deconvoluted peaks centered at 284.5 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.8 eV in the C (1s) core level spectra corresponded to the C-C-, C-O-, and C=O-related components, respectively. In contrast, the O (1s) core level confirmed the presence of O=C-related components centered at 530.8 eV. To more clearly elucidate the excited state dynamics, we measured time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) of the P-CQDs, indicating their excited state lifetimes and their slow decay process (ESI, Fig. S3). The morphological and structural characteristics of synthesized P-CQDs were recorded with a high-resolution transmission microscope (HR-TEM). The TEM images (Fig. 4a and b) revealed that the obtained P-CQDs were of spherical morphology with an average diameter of ~3 nm. Statistical analysis of the image showed that the P-CQDs fell in the ~2–4 nm size window.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing and security ink applications
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of P-CQDs can interact with metal ions through coordination chemistry. Consequently, we examined these groups against a variety of heavy metal ions with intuitiveness to develop an optical sensor (Liu et al., 2012). Sets of samples were prepared by incubating metal ions (10 ppm) and P-CQD solution at an equal ratio, and they were screened for change in fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs. In optimized conditions, a drastic decrease in P-CQDs fluorescence was observed with addition of Cu (II) ions (10 ppm). As seen in Fig. 5(a and b), there was little change in fluorescence for other metal ions. Sensing studies were conducted at physiological pH and used standard stock solutions (1000 ppm) of various ions including Cu(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), Zn(II), Mg(II), Li(II), As(III), and Co(II).It was observed that Cu (II) ions had the maximum static fluorescence quenching effect on P-CQDs fluorescence among the other metal ions. This result further indicates that these P-CQDs are more sensitive and selective for detection of Cu (II) ions than are the other metal ions. The slight decrease in intensity for Cr (III) and Pb (II) ions could have been due to non-specific interactions between these ions and P-CQDs.

The high selectivity of P-CQDs toward Cu (II) ions could have been due to selective binding of the surface carboxylate and hydroxyl functionalities of P-CQDs to Cu (II) ions, leading to formation of non-fluorescent P-CQDs-Cu (II) complexes in a ground state. Formation of these complexes was observed as a decrease in fluorescence intensity, which was found to be in proportion to the concentration of studied Cu (II) ions. Since the maximum quenching response was observed for Cu (II) ions, we studied it in detail in terms of analytical figures of merit for P-CQDs as a Cu (II) ion sensing probe. Fig. 5(c) shows the association with fluorescence response of P-CQDs against the different concentrations of Cu (II) ions in a wide window from 0.25 to 50 ppm. The figure shows a gradual decrease in PL intensity with an increase in concentration of Cu (II) ions. The regression curves displayed behavior that was calibrated and theoretically best modeled with the following equation:
y = 0.3 ∗
x
− 0.288
for
0.5 < x < 50
ppm ,
where y is F/Fo, and x is Cu (II) ion concentration (in ppm). These results reveal the applicability of the synthesized label-free chemo-probe for sensing of Cu(II) ions in a wide concentration range, while its detection occurred within the WHO guidelines of 2 ppm in drinking water (Fitzgerald, 1998). The quenching efficiency of Cu (II) ions was assessed using the Stern-Volmer equation,
I O
I
=
K SV
Q
+ C , for static quenching behavior, wherein I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of P-CQDs in the absence and presence of a quencher analyte (i.e., Cu(II)), respectively; KSV is the Stern−Volmer constant in lieu of a strong affinity between the P-CQD-Cu(II) complex; Q is the analyte (Cu (II) ions) concentration; and C is a constant. For the linear regression equation
I o
I
= 8.3 ×
10 9
+ 0.980 , Ksv was 8.3 × 109 for Cu level over 1.5–1.9 nM, as in Fig. 5(d). The basic quality assurance (QA) parameters for analysis, such as theoretical detection limit, LOD =
3.3 ∗ SD
Slope
(SD: standard deviation, i.e., 0.006 for three replicates), and quantification limit, LOQ =
10 ∗ SD
Slope
,were calculated to be 0.66 ppm and 1 ppm, respectively, both far below the WHO guidelines of 2 ppm (Fitzgerald, 1998). Table 1 shows a comparison of the analytical performance of the presented probe against earlier reported studies on CQDs for Cu (II) ion sensing in water (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2014; Ci et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2017; Rong et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2017; Qiao et al., 2018). The platform introduced in this study could also be used for detection of other metal ions by characterizing the relative quenching pattern and driving the regression behavior as well as coupling its surface with ion selective ligands, which will require further study in the future.

The quenching of P-CQDs with Cu (II) was recovered by NaHCO3, following an “Off-On” pattern. Therefore, we studied the tunable emission of green synthesized P-CQDs as a base to formulate a fluorescent ink for information encryption and decryption with multi-level protection. Rare earth metals have been conventionally used as security/fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting, encryption, and storage of information. However, carbonaceous nanostructures (carbon nitrides, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs), CQDs, etc.,) have been explored as a substitute for commercially available security ink due to their tunable optical properties and environmental friendliness (Table 2 ) (Qu et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2016; Song et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018).

To study the potential of P-CQDs as security ink, an optimized formulation of PVP:P-CQD mixture was applied to paper and photographed under visible and UV light (365 nm) (ESI, Fig. S4). Additional specific chemicals (i.e., Cu (II) at 50 ppm and sodium bicarbonate (100 mM)) were applied for encryption and decryption of the same information. The observed behavior was probed with PL and FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 6 (a) shows a decrease in fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs with addition of Cu (II) ions, with maximum quenching at a concentration of 50 ppm. This result could be attributed to the P-CQD strong chelation of Cu (II) ions through their surface functionalities by causing a surface trap of photo-excited electrons, as was observed in a sensing study (Yang et al., 2017). Notably, the fluorescence of P-CQDs was recovered (Fig. 6(b)) with addition of varied concentrations of NaHCO3 (10 mM–100 mM). This result may have been due to preferable binding of Cu (II) ions with hydroxyl ions, produced by NaHCO3 dissociation in solution, to form Cu (OH)2. As a result, the fluorescence of P-CQDs was recovered as Cu (II) ions desorbed from the P-CQDs surface. A similar effect could be attained by adding NaOH, but we observed prominent enhancement in the fluorescence by adding NaHCO3 (Qu et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2016; Song et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). The observed fluorescence quenching and recovery were supported by the change in absolute quantum yield of P-CQDs, which were observed to be 1.91% and 33.1% for encrypting and decrypting agents, respectively (ESI, Fig. S5). The results of FT-IR analysis (ESI, Fig. S6) of the complexes also supported this observation. Thus, these green synthesized P-CQDs could be a potential substitute for water-soluble fluorescent ink, upholding the privacy of information due to their switchable photoluminescence and chemical reagent-specific information encryption and decryption. The “off-on” behavior of P-CQD/PVP ink written characteristics “CSIO, CHD” was also recorded by capturing its photographic image (under UV light) with wrt Cu and NaHCO3 spray (ESI, Fig. S4). The fluorescence study showed further recovery of P-CQDs for use in the next set of “on-off” cycles. The results were well-supported by the FT-IR and quantum yield data shown in the supplementary information.

In the recent past, carbonaceous nanostructures (such as carbon nitrides, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs), CQDs) have been conventionally used as security/fluorescent ink as shown in Table 2 (Qu et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2016; Song et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). In this study, we presented for the first time green synthesized P-CQDs for current application as a substitute for chemically synthesized CQDs. Inline with previously published literature on similar applications, the results provide proof-of-concept for use of P-CQDs in security ink applications.

Furthermore, Ag@P-CQDs obtained from P-CQDs as reducing/capping agents were studied for bactericidal activity (Fig. 7(a–d))as represented by photographic images of inhibition zones wrt Ag@P-CQD concentrations against E. coli (Fig. 7(a–b)) and S. aureus (Fig. 7(c–d)) in dark and light conditions. A distinct change in diameter of the zone of inhibition was observed with wrt Ag@P-CQDs concentrations against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (ESI, Table S1). However, no significant effects of light condition were observed as reported elsewhere for CQDs, while the control (P-CQDs, active drug, and expired drug) also did not show any significant bactericidal response (despite a small effect observed for the active drug) (ESI, Fig. S7, Table S2). Upon review of the literature, the redox potential of Ag-NPs is essential for their antibacterial activity (Lok et al., 2007). However, the P-CQDs were reducing/capping agents to assist in stabilizing the oxidized state of silver in our study, thereby maintaining their antibacterial nature. The P-CQDs did not show any bactericidal response on their own. The optical density (OD) of E. coli (Fig. 7(e)) and S. aureus (Fig. 7(f))calculated from 96-well assays supported the agar diffusion assay observations. The MIC values obtained from the OD were 2 μg/mL and 5 μg/mL in light and dark conditions, respectively, wrt Ag@CQDs. Similarly, the calculated values were 3 μg/mL and 4 μg/mL, respectively, for S. aureus (ESI, Fig. S8). The difference in results in light and dark conditions for the OD and agar assay could be attributed to the diffusion properties of the drug in agar medium. The observed bactericidal activity of Ag@CQDs was assigned to the well-established toxicity of Ag-based compounds through binding with bacterial DNA and cellular proteins, primarily leading to bacterial cell death (Li et al., 2012). These multimodal applications of synthesized P-CQDs will not only help us to tackle the red category pollutants (i.e., expired pharma drugs), but also present an approach to obtain alternative green CQDs for versatile applications.

In summary, we demonstrated conversion of pharma waste (expired drugs) into value-added carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) using one-step facile pyrolysis methods for the first time. We also showed direct synthesis of Ag@P-CQDs from P-CQDs. The obtained P-CQDs and Ag@P-CQDs were demonstrated for multimodal applications including a water quality sensor for Cu (II) ions, security ink formulation, and as a bactericidal material. Therefore, the overall results of this study support the immense potential of these materials fora wide variety of applications. The proposed approach further provided new alternative ways to use pharma waste for value-added products and fits well in the waste to wealth approach. The process can be upscaled for industrial measures as a more effective pharmaceutical waste management practice than other existing methods.
Biotechnology Advances requires that all authors sign a declaration of conflicting interests. If you have nothing to declare in any of these categories then this should be stated.

All sources of funding should also be acknowledged and you should declare any involvement of study sponsors in the study design; collection, analysis and interpretation of data; the writing of the manuscript; the decision to submit the manuscript for publication. If the study sponsors had no such involvement, this should be stated.

Pooja Devi: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Neha Jindal: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ki-Hyun Kim: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Anupma Thakur: Validation, Visualization, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: A conflicting interest exists when professional judgement concerning a primary interest (such as patient's welfare or the validity of research) may be influenced by a secondary interest (such as financial gain or personal rivalry). It may arise for the authors when they have financial interest that may influence their interpretation of their results or those of others. Examples of potential conflicts of interest include employment, consultancies, stock ownership, honoraria, paid expert testimony, patent applications/registrations, and grants or other funding.

ce:section.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge the financial support of the Department of Science and Technology, India under SEED (SP/YO/126/2017) and INSPIRE fellowship grants. KHK acknowledges the support made by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, & Future Planning (Grant No: 2016R1E1A1A01940995).

PD conceptualized the idea, designed the experiments, and drafted the manuscript. AT and NJ conducted the experiments and helped in manuscript writing. KH interpreted the data and drafted the manuscript. All authors have equally contributed to the technical understanding and drafting of results. On behalf of all authors, the corresponding author states that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis absorption and XRD spectra of Ag@P-CQDs; QY and TRES of P-CQDs; “On-Off-On” behavior of P-CQDs for security ink application and the associated QY and FT-IR spectra of P-CQDs, P-CQDs-Cu (II), and the P-CQDs-Cu (II)-NaHCO3 complex; Antimicrobial activity of P-CQDs, active drugs, and expired drugs by disk diffusion method; Mean graph of optical density obtained wrt different concentrations of Ag@P-CQDs against (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.144260.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent “on-off-on” assay for selective recognition of Cu (II) and glutathione based on modified carbon nanodots, and its application to cellular imaging
